narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 65% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, based on patterns from thousands of past students. As a male, white student from an upper-middle-income family planning to study full-time, you bring several strengths like your LSAT score of 37, which is slightly above the typical student's 36.7. Your undergraduate GPA sits at 3.10, a bit below the average of 3.21, while your Year 1 school decile ranking of 5 is lower than the usual 5.71, though your Year 3 decile of 7 exceeds the average 5.53. The model's decision was influenced most strongly by your Year 3 decile ranking, which nudged the prediction toward expecting a bar failure despite being a positive mark compared to peers. Next in influence was your Year 1 decile, also pushing toward failure, followed closely by your upper-middle family income bracket. Your LSAT score and racial background as white came after those, each contributing a bit more toward the failure prediction, with your undergraduate GPA having a smaller but similar effect. Being male was one of the few factors that gently pulled the prediction toward expecting you to pass, while your full-time status had minimal sway toward failure. Overall, these academic deciles from school years stood out as the top drivers, outweighing your solid LSAT and demographic alignments that match most successful students. Compared to typical applicants, your mixed grade trajectory—starting weaker in Year 1 but improving by Year 3—didn't reassure the model enough, especially against averages where consistent performance predicts better bar outcomes. To boost your future chances, focus on building stronger academic habits now, perhaps by retaking the LSAT for an even higher score or excelling in any grad-level courses to offset past GPAs and deciles. Seeking bar prep resources early or mentorship from upper-middle-income alumni who passed could also help mimic their paths. With targeted effort on those early weaknesses, you can shift the odds in your favor next time. Hang in there—this is just one data point, and your potential shines through.
